source: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English, 2nd ed. 2015

cards.title: Narrating with full stylistic control
cards.goal: Can narrate an extended, well-formed story that weaves narrative tenses, evaluative commentary and understatement without conscious effort.
grammar: full narrative tense/aspect range deployed flexibly
understatement and litotes: not exactly ideal, hardly the best outcome
discourse: evaluative and reflective commentary woven through, not appended
cards.evidence: Tell a 3–4 minute story to an audience unfamiliar with the topic; a listener should be able to state both what happened and how the speaker felt about it without being told directly.
skin_examples: an anecdote told for effect at a social event

cards.title: Synthesising information from multiple sources
cards.goal: Can combine information and arguments from several sources into a single coherent account.
discourse: source-attribution language: according to…, X argues that…, whereas Y takes the view that…
cards.evidence: Given three short conflicting inputs (articles, opinions or reports) on one topic, deliver a two-minute synthesis that accurately represents each position and states where they diverge.
skin_examples: three reviews of the same thing
different accounts of the same event

cards.title: Describing abstract concepts and processes precisely
cards.goal: Can explain an abstract idea, system or process clearly enough for a non-specialist to follow.
grammar: nominalisation for precision: the introduction of…, the resulting shift in…
lexis: field-specific abstract vocabulary of the student's own domain
cards.evidence: Explain an abstract concept from your own field to a listener with no background in it, in under three minutes, then answer one clarifying question without oversimplifying.
skin_examples: how a professional process actually works
an abstract concept from the student's field
how a system of rules functions

cards.title: Constructing a sustained, structured argument
cards.goal: Can build a multi-point argument over several minutes, each point supporting a single overall thesis.
discourse: thesis-first structuring: my position is…, and I'll give three reasons why
cards.evidence: Deliver a 4-minute argument with an explicit thesis and three supporting points, each clearly signposted and clearly subordinate to the thesis.
skin_examples: arguing a genuinely contested professional question
a position on a social issue
defending an unpopular but reasoned view

cards.title: Conceding, qualifying and refining a position in real time
cards.goal: Can adjust an argument on the fly in response to a strong counter-point, without abandoning or contradicting the overall position.
discourse: qualifying language: that's a fair point, though I'd still argue…, to be more precise…
cards.evidence: Defend a position through three rounds of genuine pushback from the teacher, refining the claim each time without contradicting the previous round.
skin_examples: a devil's-advocate discussion on a real opinion
defending a professional decision under scrutiny
a structured debate with rebuttal rounds

cards.title: Persuasive rhetoric
cards.goal: Can use rhetorical devices deliberately to make an argument more persuasive.
cards.evidence: Deliver a 90-second persuasive appeal using at least one rhetorical question, one three-part list and one instance of deliberate repetition.

cards.title: Fine-grading certainty and commitment
cards.goal: Can signal precise degrees of certainty, commitment and distance from a claim.
grammar: epistemic modality: might well, could conceivably, it's fair to say, I wouldn't go so far as to say
cards.evidence: Respond to five claims of varying certainty, matching your own certainty precisely with an appropriate hedge each time — including at least one full commitment and one strong distancing.

cards.title: Implication, inference & reading between the lines
cards.goal: Can imply meaning without stating it directly, and correctly infer implied meaning from others.
discourse: implicature: I'm not saying X, but…, let's just say…, make of that what you will
cards.evidence: Given five short exchanges where speaker intent is only implied, correctly state what each speaker actually meant; then produce two implied statements of your own for the teacher to interpret.

cards.title: Irony, understatement & downplaying
cards.goal: Can recognise and use irony and understatement appropriately for tone.
discourse: ironic markers: yeah, that went well…, no rush or anything…
cards.evidence: Identify the intended tone in five ironic or understated statements (serious vs joking vs frustrated), then produce two of your own that a listener correctly reads as ironic.

cards.title: Structuring a formal presentation or report
cards.goal: Can structure an extended formal talk or report with clear signposting at the level of whole sections, not just sentences.
discourse: macro-signposting: in the first part of this talk…, having established X, I'll now turn to…, I want to draw these threads together
cards.evidence: Deliver a structured 5-minute talk with at least three clearly signposted sections and an explicit closing synthesis, not just a summary of points.
skin_examples: a conference-style talk on a familiar topic

cards.title: Nominalisation & formal written register
cards.goal: Can shift between a spoken, verb-heavy style and a formal, noun-heavy written style as the situation demands.
grammar: nominalisation: we decided to reduce costs → the decision to reduce costs
formal connectors: furthermore, consequently, with regard to, in light of
cards.evidence: Take five spoken, informal sentences and rework them into a formal written register using nominalisation and formal connectors, preserving the full meaning.
skin_examples: turning a verbal update into a written report

cards.title: Advanced cohesion across a long stretch
cards.goal: Can maintain clear reference and avoid repetition across an extended piece of discourse.
grammar: substitution and ellipsis: so do I, I think so, the former/the latter
complex reference tracking across several sentences
cards.evidence: Deliver a 3-minute monologue on a topic with several recurring entities, using substitution/ellipsis at least three times instead of repeating full noun phrases.
skin_examples: a story with several returning characters

cards.title: Chairing or facilitating a discussion
cards.goal: Can manage a group discussion: invite contributions, redirect a tangent, and summarise before moving on.
discourse: facilitation language: what's your take on this…, let's park that for now…, so to summarise before we move on…
cards.evidence: Chair a 5-minute multi-person discussion: draw in a quiet participant, redirect one tangent, and summarise the discussion before closing it.

cards.title: Diplomatic disagreement & face-saving language
cards.goal: Can disagree firmly with someone of higher status or in a sensitive situation without causing offence.
discourse: face-saving language: I hear what you're saying, and I wonder if there's another way of looking at it…, with respect…, I don't think that's quite right, actually
cards.evidence: Disagree with a status-asymmetric roleplay partner (a superior, a difficult client) on a substantive point, holding the position while maintaining visible respect throughout.
skin_examples: pushing back on a client's assumption

cards.title: Reformulating on the fly for precision
cards.goal: Can notice an imprecise or ambiguous own utterance and repair it immediately without losing fluency.
discourse: reformulation markers: or rather, what I should say is, to put it more precisely, in other words
cards.evidence: In a 3-minute extemporaneous talk, self-correct at least twice using a reformulation marker, each time genuinely sharpening the meaning rather than just restarting.
skin_examples: clarifying a claim under gentle challenge

cards.title: Giving and receiving detailed, nuanced feedback
cards.goal: Can give feedback that is honest, specific and constructive, and receive critical feedback gracefully.
exponents: One thing that really worked was…, though I did wonder about…
That's fair — I hadn't considered that
cards.evidence: Give structured feedback on a piece of work (one strength, one specific improvement, one question), then receive critical feedback on something of your own and respond constructively rather than defensively.
skin_examples: feedback on a pitch or plan
peer review of a piece of writing

cards.title: Sophisticated negotiation
cards.goal: Can negotiate trade-offs and conditional offers over multiple rounds toward a favourable outcome.
grammar: conditional offers: we'd be prepared to X, provided that Y
cards.evidence: Run a 3-round negotiation roleplay with genuine competing interests, using at least two conditional offers and reaching (or deliberately walking away from) a deal, justified either way.

cards.title: Handling Q&A under pressure
cards.goal: Can field challenging, sceptical or hostile questions after a talk without losing composure or precision.
discourse: stalling and bridging language: that's a fair challenge — let me address that directly, I'd push back slightly on the premise there
cards.evidence: After a short prepared talk, field three deliberately difficult or sceptical questions from the teacher, answering each directly without deflecting or losing the thread.
skin_examples: defending a decision to sceptical stakeholders

cards.title: Idiomatic and collocational range in professional contexts
cards.goal: Can use natural collocations and idiomatic expressions appropriate to a professional register.
lexis: professional collocations and idiom: hit the ground running, a steep learning curve, get the ball rolling, in the pipeline
cards.evidence: Use six professional collocations/idioms accurately and naturally within a 3-minute discussion of real work or study, without forcing them in.
skin_examples: talking about starting a new role

cards.title: Advanced grammar for stylistic effect
cards.goal: Can deploy marked grammatical structures deliberately, for emphasis or formality, rather than by default.
grammar: inversion after negative adverbials: Not until…, Under no circumstances…, Rarely, Little did…
formal subjunctive: it is essential that he be informed
cards.evidence: Produce four sentences using marked structures from the list above, each in a context where the emphasis or formality is clearly appropriate to the situation, not just grammatically correct.
skin_examples: a formal warning or policy statement
